senses.gloss: ink
beauty
to make happy; to elate
to ink, to fill, cover, smear, mark, write, draw, illustrate, or outline with ink
to ink, to fill, cover, smear, mark, write, draw, illustrate, or outline with ink; to write
to improve the overall quality of (a text, a performance, a skill, and so on), to refine, to polish; to perfect

to improve the shape, form, style, structure, or composition of (handwriting, voice, and so on), to beautify; to embellish; to pretty; to improve in aspect or appearance, to make visually pleasing, to deck, to decorate, to ornament
to improve the shape, form, style, structure, or composition of (handwriting, voice, and so on), to beautify; to embellish; to pretty; to pattern (a garment); to embroider
to be or become glad or happy, to cheer up, to brighten
to become discolored (as if stained with ink), to become dark, to yellow
verbal noun of حَبَرَ (ḥabara) (form I)
to ink, to fill, cover, smear, mark, write, draw, illustrate, or outline with ink
to ink, to fill, cover, smear, mark, write, draw, illustrate, or outline with ink; to write (especially in an elegant style), to compose
to better or improve in quality, appearance, etc. to enhance; to polish up; to perfect
to better or improve in quality, appearance, etc. to enhance; to polish up; to perfect; to prettify; to bedeck, to adorn
a scholar (especially one that is highly regarded and respected by a certain group and is deemed to be an authority)

a well-versed religious scribe; a learned theologian; a religious authority; a bishop

a well-versed religious scribe; a learned theologian; a religious authority; an esteemed authoritative theologian, an imam

etymology.text:
Borrowed from Classical Syriac ܚܒܪܐ (ḥeḇrā, “ink”), from the root ܚ ܒ ܪ related to darkness. Merged with the root ح ب ر (ḥ b r).

Senses related to ink, writing, and discoloration are from Etymology 1 (see above).

Denominal verb of حِبْر (ḥibr, “ink”). Senses related to improvement, perfection, and embellishment are from or influenced by the root ح ب ر (ḥ b r) (see above).

Borrowed from Biblical Hebrew חָבֵר (ḥɔḇer, “comrade”) in a specific meaning as used for sectarian cadres. Merged with Etymology 3 by analogy with the use of ink by religious clerks and scribes, hence "a man of learning", hence "a scholarly theologian", hence "an authority in matters of religion".
